A powerful Nor'easter traveled across Cape Cod into the Gulf of Maine on January 12th with portions of northern New York on the northwest outskirts of this storm. ||This storm had a long, dangerous and devastating wintry history from the southern Plains and Gulf coast states on January 9th to the southeast United States on January 10th, then coastal mid-Atlantic on January 11th until impacting the northeast late January 11th into January 12th.||Snow began across portions of northern New York around daybreak and continued nearly until midnight. Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches along the Canadian border to a foot in the higher elevations of Essex county. Some specific snowfall totals include; 15 inches in Whiteface Mountain, 10-13 inches in Lake Placid, 8 inches in Ticonderoga.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 15 inches of snow fell across western Essex county, with some specific snowfall totals including; 15 inches at Whiteface Mountain with 10 to 13 inches across Lake Placid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of the Lower Hudson Valley. This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  With water temperatures in the lower 60s, much of Long Island received mainly rainfall and up to 2 inches of rain during the event.  Areas just north and west of New York City, however, were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell across interior portions of the Lower Hudson Valley, with one foot or more of snowfall across highest elevations.  In addition to the heavy rain and snow...strong winds were experienced along the immediate coastline. ||Thousands of people across southeast New York lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn. This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and power outages occurred due to 2 to 4 inches of heavy wet snow. A significant number of trees came down across the county starting around 1 pm EST due to the combined heavy wet snow and 15 to 20 mph winds. Hundreds of trees and thousands of tree limbs were downed through the storm, especially in Central Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system originating from the deep south brought heavy snow and sleet to east central New York. Initially light snow overspread the area as a result of a weak area of low pressure moving northeastward off the mid Atlantic and northeast coasts on Tuesday, February 1st.  A much stronger low approached from the Ohio Valley Tuesday night and crossed the region on Wednesday, February 2nd.||Snowfall reports across east central New York ranged from as little 4 inches up to 15 inches with a majority of reports falling between 8 and 12 inches.||The heavy wet snow resulted in some roof collapses. In Northumberland in Saratoga County, half of the roof of a barn collapsed at a dairy farm; unconfirmed reports were about 200 cows were in the barn and nearly 30 of them were killed. In Greenwich in Washington County, a heifer barn on a family farm collapsed trapping 3 or 4 animals. In Albany, the roof collapsed at a storage garage for a roofing company.  ||Snow emergencies were declared in the City of Albany, City of Amsterdam, Village of Athens, Town of Cairo, Village of Castleton-on-the-Hudson, Village of Catskill, Town of Coeymans, Town of Cohoes, Town of Colonie, Columbia County, Dalton, Town of East Greenbush, City of Glens Falls, Village of Green Island, Village of Hudson Falls, City of Mechanicville, Village of Menands, City of Poughkeepsie, Village of Ravena, City of Rensselaer, City of Saratoga Springs, Town of Schodack, Village of Schuylerville, Village of Stillwater, City of Troy and Village of Waterford.

Low pressure tracked northeast from the southern Plains into the Ohio Valley Thursday February 24th, then from the Ohio Valley across Pennsylvania to Long Island during Friday February 25th, before tracking rapidly northeast off the New England coast Friday night.||This storm system produced a widespread swath of heavy wet snow across the greater Capital District and surrounding area, the Lake George Saratoga region, the Mohawk River Valley, Schoharie Valley and southern Adirondacks during the day Friday. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred, beginning during the early morning hours, and persisting until late afternoon.||Snowfall amounts reached 12 to 15 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region extending into the east central Mohawk River Valley and Lake George Saratoga region, with generally 8 to 12 inches across southern portions of the Capital Region and eastern Catskills.||The heavy snow created treacherous travel conditions for the morning and evening commutes on Friday, with numerous accidents reported, including along portions of the Adirondack Northway, as well as Interstate 90. The heavy wet snow also led to numerous school and business closings across much of eastern New York on Friday.

Low pressure tracked northeast from the southern Plains into the Ohio Valley Thursday February 24th, then from the Ohio Valley across Pennsylvania to Long Island during Friday February 25th, before tracking rapidly northeast off the New England coast Friday night.||This storm system produced a widespread swath of heavy wet snow across the greater Capital District and surrounding area, the Lake George Saratoga region, the Mohawk River Valley, Schoharie Valley and southern Adirondacks during the day Friday. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred, beginning during the early morning hours, and persisting until late afternoon.||Snowfall amounts reached 12 to 15 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region extending into the east central Mohawk River Valley and Lake George Saratoga region, with generally 8 to 12 inches across southern portions of the Capital Region and eastern Catskills.||The heavy snow created treacherous travel conditions for the morning and evening commutes on Friday, with numerous accidents reported, including along portions of the Adirondack Northway, as well as Interstate 90. The heavy wet snow also led to numerous school and business closings across much of eastern New York on Friday.

The combination of the passage of an arctic cold frontal boundary, and the development of a coastal low just of the mid Atlantic coast brought a moderate snowfall to the northern Taconics, eastern Rensselaer County. Snowfall reports ranged from 4 to 10 inches.

An arctic cold front moved across the region early in the morning Saturday, February 19th. A tight pressure gradient developed across the region in the wake of the boundary resulting in very strong and gusty west to northwest winds during the day. Wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph were reported across east central New York. Trees, limbs and wires were reported down along with widespread power outages. The highest concentration of power outages occurred across the higher terrain of the eastern Catskills in Green and Ulster counties. At Windham Mountain in the Catskills, a power outage left some skiers stuck on the ski lift for a time.||Due to the high winds, the New York State Thruway Authority temporarily prohibited tandem tractor-trailers between Exit 25A (Schenectady Interstate 88) west to Exit 47 (Rocchester-LeRoy).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down and a roof blown off a sugar house in Halcott Center due to winds.

Irene made landfall locally as a tropical storm around 900 am August 28, 2011 over New York City.  Irene then moved across Southeastern New York and Western Connecticut before dissipating over Northern New England near the Canadian Border the evening of August 28. The large envelope of winds associated with Tropical Storm Irene pushed a 3 to 5 foot surge of water along Western Long Island Sound, New York Harbor, the southern and eastern bays of Long Island and southern bays of New York City. This resulted in moderate to major coastal flooding, wave damage and erosion along the coast, with heavy damage to public beaches and other public and private facilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tidal gauge at the Battery recorded a maximum water level of 9.50 ft. MLLW at 842 am on August 28 2011, which is well above the NWS threshold for moderate coastal flooding.||These water levels caused widespread moderate to major coastal flooding.  In the Red Hook section of Brooklyn, water inundated about 100 yards inland along portions of Gowanus Bay/Canal.  In the Battery Park City section of Manhattan, 1 to 2 ft. of inundation occurred along the promenade.  In addition, 1 foot of water inundation was experienced over the marina in front of the Mercatile Exchange.  On the East Side of Manhattan, the combination of heavy rain and storm surge caused inundation of a few feet of water onto both directions of the FDR at Houston Street and necessitated its closure. About a foot of water inundated the streets in South Street Seaport. On the west side of Manhattan, the southbound Henry Hudson Drive was closed in Harlem due to combined heavy rain and storm surge causing 3 feet of water to stand over the roadway.  Similarly, the north part of the Holland Tunnel was closed due to heavy rain and storm surge flooding.  Also the West Village Houses on the southern end of 10th Ave. were closed with 2 cars stuck due to heavy rain and storm surge flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across Clinton county were 10 to 20 inches.

Low pressure tracked from the eastern Gulf Coast toward the mid Atlantic Coast Friday February 4th, then from the mid Atlantic Coast toward the New York City area during Saturday February 5th. This low, in combination with a weaker surface low tracking northeast from the Tennessee Valley produced a swath of mixed precipitation across much of eastern New York during Saturday.||A mix of snow and sleet developed late Saturday morning across the southeast Catskills, before changing to mainly freezing rain and sleet by late afternoon.||Across the greater Capital District, the Mohawk Valley region, the Lake George Saratoga region and the southern Adirondacks, a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed by early afternoon, and continued into Saturday evening. The precipitation became mainly freezing rain and sleet across the Capital District, with a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain persisting further north.||The precipitation changed back to snow across much of the region before ending late Saturday night.||Snow and sleet amounts generally remained under one inch across the immediate Capital District southward, but increased rapidly further north and west, with 1 to 3 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region, and 3 to 6 inches across much of the eastern and central Mohawk River Valley, the Lake George region, and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice accretion of between one and two tenths of an inch occurred in many areas of eastern New York during Saturday afternoon and evening.||Furthermore, a line of thunderstorms accompanied by frequent cloud to ground lightning and small hail moved across eastern New York from the Saratoga region southward into the mid Hudson Valley during Saturday evening, with pea to dime size hail reported in many areas while freezing rain also occurred.||The wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions across much of east central New York Saturday afternoon and evening, with numerous traffic accidents reported.

A major winter storm developed in the southern Plains and western Gulf coast states on February 1st and proceeded to move into the Ohio River Valley on the morning of February 2nd. On the morning of February 2nd, a coastal low developed off the Delmarva peninsula and became the main storm as it tracked near Cape Cod toward Nova Scotia during the afternoon.||Snow overspread northern New York in the early morning hours of February 2nd and fell heavy at times, with snowfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour at times, before ending during the evening. Snowfall totals of 10 to 20 inches were observed across northern New York, including 20 inches in Peru (Clinton county), 15 inches in Schuyler Falls (Clinton county), 13 inches in Moriah (Essex county), 12 inches in St. Regis Falls (Franklin county) and 10 inches in Edwards (St. Lawrence county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across St. Lawrence county were 8 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 5th, an active storm system developed and moved across the Gulf coast states. This storm produced severe weather, including deadly tornadoes along the Gulf coast and major flooding across the Tennessee River Valley. In New York, a cold front was draped across western New York with rainfall immediately along the cold front and temperatures in the 40s across northern New York that promoted significant snow melt to the region. ||On March 6th, this storm began to move north across the southeast and Mid-Atlantic states along a slow moving cold front positioned across New York south to Florida. More heavy rainfall, flooding and severe weather accompanied this system. In northern New York, heavy rainfall and temperatures in the 40s preceded the cold front. However, as the cold front crept across northern New York during the morning hours, temperatures fell 12-15 degrees within an hour and heavy rain changed to heavy sleet and wet snow. ||At the same time, rapid snow melt and heavy rainfall accounted for area ice-covered rivers to swell and cause ice flows. A significant ice jam formed in the Keene Valley between Jay and Keene that resulted in moderate to major flooding of numerous homes and forced evacuations during a major snowstorm. ||Heavy snow continued from the afternoon of March 6th to midday on March 7th with the heaviest occurring in the early morning hours of the 7th with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches in the St. Lawrence River Valley to 18 to 30 inches across much of the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley.||Most roads were impassable with numerous accidents and stuck vehicles. Nearly 5000 customers lost electrical power, nearly all school districts were closed on the 7th along with local/state government as State of Emergencies were declared in Clinton and Essex counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 20 to 32 inches were reported in Essex county, including; 32 inches in Bloomingdale, 27 inches in Westport and 23 inches in Lake Placid and Moriah.

A complex low pressure system originating from the deep south brought heavy snow and sleet to east central New York. Initially light snow overspread the area as a result of a weak area of low pressure moving northeastward off the mid Atlantic and northeast coasts on Tuesday, February 1st.  A much stronger low approached from the Ohio Valley Tuesday night and crossed the region on Wednesday, February 2nd.||Snowfall reports across east central New York ranged from as little 4 inches up to 15 inches with a majority of reports falling between 8 and 12 inches.||The heavy wet snow resulted in some roof collapses. Low pressure developed along the southeast coast Tuesday, January 11th, and explosive deepening, cyclogenesis, occurred Tuesday night as the low tracked northeastward. The storm passed over eastern Long Island and Cape Cod Wednesday morning, January 12th, then continued to strengthen during the day as it gradually moved up the New England coast.||Moderate to heavy snow fell across east central New York, with snowfall amounts  ranging from as little as 4 inches across portions of the western Adirondacks and the western and central Mohawk Valley, up to almost 2 feet across portions of the Taconics. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches an hour occurred across portions of east central New York, mainly to the east of the Hudson River Valley.||It was reported that 21 departing and 11 arriving flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport early Wednesday morning. Also, the Capital District Transit Authority, CDTA, reported system-wide delays. ||Snow emergencies were declared in the City of Albany, Village of Athens, Town of  Cairo, Village of Castleton, Village of Catskill, Town of East Greenbush, Village of Green Island, Town of Greenport, City of Hudson, City of Mechanicville, Village of Menands, City of Poughkeepsie, City of Saratoga Springs, City of Schenectady, Town of Schodack, Village of Stillwater, Town of Waterford and City of Watervliet.

The combination of showers and thunderstorms in association with a slow moving cold front and deep tropical moisture moving up the east coast from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee, produced a prolonged period of rainfall, which led to flooding across portions of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Washingtonville, Democracy Ln. and Freedom Dr. were closed due to flooding.  Mandatory evacuations were ordered in Washingtonville Manor and Brookside Acres as well as on Patricia Ln., Beverly Ln., Cardinal Dr., Peacock Circle and W. Main St. due to flooding of Moodna Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 5th, an active storm system developed and moved across the Gulf coast states. This storm produced severe weather, including deadly tornadoes along the Gulf coast and major flooding across the Tennessee River Valley. In New York, a cold front was draped across western New York with rainfall immediately along the cold front and temperatures in the 40s across northern New York that promoted significant snow melt to the region. ||On March 6th, this storm began to move north across the southeast and Mid-Atlantic states along a slow moving cold front positioned across New York south to Florida. More heavy rainfall, flooding and severe weather accompanied this system. In northern New York, heavy rainfall and temperatures in the 40s preceded the cold front. However, as the cold front crept across northern New York during the morning hours, temperatures fell 12-15 degrees within an hour and heavy rain changed to heavy sleet and wet snow. ||At the same time, rapid snow melt and heavy rainfall accounted for area ice-covered rivers to swell and cause ice flows. A significant ice jam formed in the Keene Valley between Jay and Keene that resulted in moderate to major flooding of numerous homes and forced evacuations during a major snowstorm. ||Heavy snow continued from the afternoon of March 6th to midday on March 7th with the heaviest occurring in the early morning hours of the 7th with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches in the St. Lawrence River Valley to 18 to 30 inches across much of the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley.||Most roads were impassable with numerous accidents and stuck vehicles. Nearly 5000 customers lost electrical power, nearly all school districts were closed on the 7th along with local/state government as State of Emergencies were declared in Clinton and Essex counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 18 to 24 inches were reported in Clinton county, including; 24 inches in Peru, 22 inches in Plattsburgh and Morrisonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northeast from the southern Plains into the Ohio Valley Thursday February 24th, then from the Ohio Valley across Pennsylvania to Long Island during Friday February 25th, before tracking rapidly northeast off the New England coast Friday night.||This storm system produced a widespread swath of heavy wet snow across the greater Capital District and surrounding area, the Lake George Saratoga region, the Mohawk River Valley, Schoharie Valley and southern Adirondacks during the day Friday. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred, beginning during the early morning hours, and persisting until late afternoon.||Snowfall amounts reached 12 to 15 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region extending into the east central Mohawk River Valley and Lake George Saratoga region, with generally 8 to 12 inches across southern portions of the Capital Region and eastern Catskills.||The heavy snow created treacherous travel conditions for the morning and evening commutes on Friday, with numerous accidents reported, including along portions of the Adirondack Northway, as well as Interstate 90. The heavy wet snow also led to numerous school and business closings across much of eastern New York on Friday.

An arctic cold front moved across the region early in the morning Saturday, February 19th. A tight pressure gradient developed across the region in the wake of the boundary resulting in very strong and gusty west to northwest winds during the day. Wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph were reported across east central New York. Trees, limbs and wires were reported down along with widespread power outages. The highest concentration of power outages occurred across the higher terrain of the eastern Catskills in Green and Ulster counties. At Windham Mountain in the Catskills, a power outage left some skiers stuck on the ski lift for a time.||Due to the high winds, the New York State Thruway Authority temporarily prohibited tandem tractor-trailers between Exit 25A (Schenectady Interstate 88) west to Exit 47 (Rocchester-LeRoy).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Siding and flashing were reported torn off an apartment complex building in North Greenbush due to the wind. Also, large tree limbs were reported downed by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along mid Atlantic coast Tuesday, January 18th, and strengthened as it moved northeastward passing over Cape Cod Tuesday night around midnight. This storm brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the area. ||Snow and sleet accumulations across east central New York varied from only an inch or two across the mid Hudson Valley, with up to 7 to 11 inches across southern Adirondacks. Ice accumulations of a tenth of an inch, up to a half of an inch were reported, with the highest amounts across portions of the mid Hudson Valley. The snow and ice resulted in power outages, especially in the mid Hudson Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system originating from the deep south brought heavy snow and sleet to east central New York. Initially light snow overspread the area as a result of a weak area of low pressure moving northeastward off the mid Atlantic and northeast coasts on Tuesday, February 1st.  A much stronger low approached from the Ohio Valley Tuesday night and crossed the region on Wednesday, February 2nd.||Snowfall reports across east central New York ranged from as little 4 inches up to 15 inches with a majority of reports falling between 8 and 12 inches.||The heavy wet snow resulted in some roof collapses. In Northumberland in Saratoga County, half of the roof of a barn collapsed at a dairy farm; unconfirmed reports were about 200 cows were in the barn and nearly 30 of them were killed. In Greenwich in Washington County, a heifer barn on a family farm collapsed trapping 3 or 4 animals. In Albany, the roof collapsed at a storage garage for a roofing company.  ||Snow emergencies were declared in the City of Albany, City of Amsterdam, Village of Athens, Town of Cairo, Village of Castleton-on-the-Hudson, Village of Catskill, Town of Coeymans, Town of Cohoes, Town of Colonie, Columbia County, Dalton, Town of East Greenbush, City of Glens Falls, Village of Green Island, Village of Hudson Falls, City of Mechanicville, Village of Menands, City of Poughkeepsie, Village of Ravena, City of Rensselaer, City of Saratoga Springs, Town of Schodack, Village of Schuylerville, Village of Stillwater, City of Troy and Village of Waterford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the eastern Gulf Coast toward the mid Atlantic Coast Friday February 4th, then from the mid Atlantic Coast toward the New York City area during Saturday February 5th. This low, in combination with a weaker surface low tracking northeast from the Tennessee Valley produced a swath of mixed precipitation across much of eastern New York during Saturday.||A mix of snow and sleet developed late Saturday morning across the southeast Catskills, before changing to mainly freezing rain and sleet by late afternoon.||Across the greater Capital District, the Mohawk Valley region, the Lake George Saratoga region and the southern Adirondacks, a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed by early afternoon, and continued into Saturday evening. The precipitation became mainly freezing rain and sleet across the Capital District, with a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain persisting further north.||The precipitation changed back to snow across much of the region before ending late Saturday night.||Snow and sleet amounts generally remained under one inch across the immediate Capital District southward, but increased rapidly further north and west, with 1 to 3 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region, and 3 to 6 inches across much of the eastern and central Mohawk River Valley, the Lake George region, and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice accretion of between one and two tenths of an inch occurred in many areas of eastern New York during Saturday afternoon and evening.||Furthermore, a line of thunderstorms accompanied by frequent cloud to ground lightning and small hail moved across eastern New York from the Saratoga region southward into the mid Hudson Valley during Saturday evening, with pea to dime size hail reported in many areas while freezing rain also occurred.||The wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions across much of east central New York Saturday afternoon and evening, with numerous traffic accidents reported.

The precipitation became mainly freezing rain and sleet across the Capital District, with a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain persisting further north.||The precipitation changed back to snow across much of the region before ending late Saturday night.||Snow and sleet amounts generally remained under one inch across the immediate Capital District southward, but increased rapidly further north and west, with 1 to 3 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region, and 3 to 6 inches across much of the eastern and central Mohawk River Valley, the Lake George region, and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice accretion of between one and two tenths of an inch occurred in many areas of eastern New York during Saturday afternoon and evening.||Furthermore, a line of thunderstorms accompanied by frequent cloud to ground lightning and small hail moved across eastern New York from the Saratoga region southward into the mid Hudson Valley during Saturday evening, with pea to dime size hail reported in many areas while freezing rain also occurred.||The wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions across much of east central New York Saturday afternoon and evening, with numerous traffic accidents reported.

A complex low pressure system originating from the deep south brought heavy snow and sleet to east central New York. Initially light snow overspread the area as a result of a weak area of low pressure moving northeastward off the mid Atlantic and northeast coasts on Tuesday, February 1st.  A much stronger low approached from the Ohio Valley Tuesday night and crossed the region on Wednesday, February 2nd.||Snowfall reports across east central New York ranged from as little 4 inches up to 15 inches with a majority of reports falling between 8 and 12 inches.||The heavy wet snow resulted in some roof collapses. In Northumberland in Saratoga County, half of the roof of a barn collapsed at a dairy farm; unconfirmed reports were about 200 cows were in the barn and nearly 30 of them were killed. In Greenwich in Washington County, a heifer barn on a family farm collapsed trapping 3 or 4 animals. In Albany, the roof collapsed at a storage garage for a roofing company.  ||Snow emergencies were declared in the City of Albany, City of Amsterdam, Village of Athens, Town of Cairo, Village of Castleton-on-the-Hudson, Village of Catskill, Town of Coeymans, Town of Cohoes, Town of Colonie, Columbia County, Dalton, Town of East Greenbush, City of Glens Falls, Village of Green Island, Village of Hudson Falls, City of Mechanicville, Village of Menands, City of Poughkeepsie, Village of Ravena, City of Rensselaer, City of Saratoga Springs, Town of Schodack, Village of Schuylerville, Village of Stillwater, City of Troy and Village of Waterford.

A storm that brought severe weather to portions of the Mississippi River Valley on February 24th was located in the Ohio River Valley on the morning of the 25th and into the Gulf of Maine by that evening. ||Snow overspread northern New York by daybreak on the 25th and fell heavy at times, with snowfall rates of 2+ inches an hour in southern Franklin and Essex counties before ending by evening. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches along the Canadian border with 8 to 12 inches in the southern Adirondacks and southern Champlain Valley. Some specific totals include; 13 inches in Lake Placid (Franklin county), 10 inches in Tupper Lake (Franklin county), 8 inches in Peru (Clinton county) and 6 inches in Edwards (St. Lawrence county).

The combination of showers and thunderstorms in association with a slow moving cold front and deep tropical moisture moving up the east coast from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee, produced a prolonged period of rainfall, which led to flooding across portions of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire length of the Bronx River Pkwy. and Saw Mill River Pkwy. were closed due to flooding. The exit ramps on Interstate-95 in Mamaroneck were also closed due to flooding.  Total reported rainfall amounts in Westchester County ranged from 5.14 inches in Thornwood to 6.80 inches in White Plains.

On March 5th, an active storm system developed and moved across the Gulf coast states. This storm produced severe weather, including deadly tornadoes along the Gulf coast and major flooding across the Tennessee River Valley. In New York, a cold front was draped across western New York with rainfall immediately along the cold front and temperatures in the 40s across northern New York that promoted significant snow melt to the region. ||On March 6th, this storm began to move north across the southeast and Mid-Atlantic states along a slow moving cold front positioned across New York south to Florida. More heavy rainfall, flooding and severe weather accompanied this system. In northern New York, heavy rainfall and temperatures in the 40s preceded the cold front. However, as the cold front crept across northern New York during the morning hours, temperatures fell 12-15 degrees within an hour and heavy rain changed to heavy sleet and wet snow. ||At the same time, rapid snow melt and heavy rainfall accounted for area ice-covered rivers to swell and cause ice flows. A significant ice jam formed in the Keene Valley between Jay and Keene that resulted in moderate to major flooding of numerous homes and forced evacuations during a major snowstorm. ||Heavy snow continued from the afternoon of March 6th to midday on March 7th with the heaviest occurring in the early morning hours of the 7th with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches in the St. Lawrence River Valley to 18 to 30 inches across much of the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley.||Most roads were impassable with numerous accidents and stuck vehicles. Nearly 5000 customers lost electrical power, nearly all school districts were closed on the 7th along with local/state government as State of Emergencies were declared in Clinton and Essex counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches were reported in St. Lawrence county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of the Lower Hudson Valley. This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  With water temperatures in the lower 60s, much of Long Island received mainly rainfall and up to 2 inches of rain during the event.  Areas just north and west of New York City, however, were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell across interior portions of the Lower Hudson Valley, with one foot or more of snowfall across highest elevations.  In addition to the heavy rain and snow...strong winds were experienced along the immediate coastline. ||Thousands of people across southeast New York lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn. This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and power outages occurred due to 6 to 10 inches of heavy wet snow. The heavier amounts fell in the higher elevations and away from the coast. In White Plains, a large tree limb was downed on Anderson Hill Road around 1243 pm EST, partially blocking it. Additionally, numerous trees and wires were reported down in Vernon Park and Mamaroneck between 2 and 3 pm EST.

This low, in combination with a weaker surface low tracking northeast from the Tennessee Valley produced a swath of mixed precipitation across much of eastern New York during Saturday.||A mix of snow and sleet developed late Saturday morning across the southeast Catskills, before changing to mainly freezing rain and sleet by late afternoon.||Across the greater Capital District, the Mohawk Valley region, the Lake George Saratoga region and the southern Adirondacks, a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed by early afternoon, and continued into Saturday evening. The precipitation became mainly freezing rain and sleet across the Capital District, with a mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain persisting further north.||The precipitation changed back to snow across much of the region before ending late Saturday night.||Snow and sleet amounts generally remained under one inch across the immediate Capital District southward, but increased rapidly further north and west, with 1 to 3 inches across northern portions of the Capital Region, and 3 to 6 inches across much of the eastern and central Mohawk River Valley, the Lake George region, and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice accretion of between one and two tenths of an inch occurred in many areas of eastern New York during Saturday afternoon and evening.||Furthermore, a line of thunderstorms accompanied by frequent cloud to ground lightning and small hail moved across eastern New York from the Saratoga region southward into the mid Hudson Valley during Saturday evening, with pea to dime size hail reported in many areas while freezing rain also occurred.||The wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions across much of east central New York Saturday afternoon and evening, with numerous traffic accidents reported.

On March 5th, an active storm system developed and moved across the Gulf coast states. This storm produced severe weather, including deadly tornadoes along the Gulf coast and major flooding across the Tennessee River Valley. In New York, a cold front was draped across western New York with rainfall immediately along the cold front and temperatures in the 40s across northern New York that promoted significant snow melt to the region. ||On March 6th, this storm began to move north across the southeast and Mid-Atlantic states along a slow moving cold front positioned across New York south to Florida. More heavy rainfall, flooding and severe weather accompanied this system. In northern New York, heavy rainfall and temperatures in the 40s preceded the cold front. However, as the cold front crept across northern New York during the morning hours, temperatures fell 12-15 degrees within an hour and heavy rain changed to heavy sleet and wet snow. ||At the same time, rapid snow melt and heavy rainfall accounted for area ice-covered rivers to swell and cause ice flows. A significant ice jam formed in the Keene Valley between Jay and Keene that resulted in moderate to major flooding of numerous homes and forced evacuations during a major snowstorm. ||Heavy snow continued from the afternoon of March 6th to midday on March 7th with the heaviest occurring in the early morning hours of the 7th with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches in the St. Lawrence River Valley to 18 to 30 inches across much of the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley.||Most roads were impassable with numerous accidents and stuck vehicles. Nearly 5000 customers lost electrical power, nearly all school districts were closed on the 7th along with local/state government as State of Emergencies were declared in Clinton and Essex counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 18 to 24 inches were reported in Clinton county, including; 24 inches in Peru, 22 inches in Plattsburgh and Morrisonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of the Lower Hudson Valley. This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  With water temperatures in the lower 60s, much of Long Island received mainly rainfall and up to 2 inches of rain during the event.  Areas just north and west of New York City, however, were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell across interior portions of the Lower Hudson Valley, with one foot or more of snowfall across highest elevations.  In addition to the heavy rain and snow...strong winds were experienced along the immediate coastline. ||Thousands of people across southeast New York lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn. This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant number of trees came down across the county starting around 1 pm EST due to the combination of 1 to 3 inches of heavy wet snow and 15 to 20 mph winds.  Hundreds of trees were downed through the storm.

In New City, downed trees blocked lanes on route 304 at Bardonia and Germonds around 230 pm EST. Meanwhile, numerous trees and tree limbs were downed in Tallman and Pomona by 4 pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm dumped wet snow across the Catskills, as deepening low pressure moved up the coast. Snow amounts were very elevation-dependent. Some valleys, especially the Delaware, had only a few inches of snow at lower elevations, yet the Catskills in some cases had more than a foot of snow at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches or so of heavy wet snow dumped on Delaware County, with locally higher amounts at highest elevations. Arkville had 7.8 inches.  In the valleys, accumulations were limited to 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system originating from the deep south brought heavy snow and sleet to east central New York. Initially light snow overspread the area as a result of a weak area of low pressure moving northeastward off the mid Atlantic and northeast coasts on Tuesday, February 1st.  A much stronger low approached from the Ohio Valley Tuesday night and crossed the region on Wednesday, February 2nd.||Snowfall reports across east central New York ranged from as little 4 inches up to 15 inches with a majority of reports falling between 8 and 12 inches.||The heavy wet snow resulted in some roof collapses. In Northumberland in Saratoga County, half of the roof of a barn collapsed at a dairy farm; unconfirmed reports were about 200 cows were in the barn and nearly 30 of them were killed. In Greenwich in Washington County, a heifer barn on a family farm collapsed trapping 3 or 4 animals. In Albany, the roof collapsed at a storage garage for a roofing company.  ||Snow emergencies were declared in the City of Albany, City of Amsterdam, Village of Athens, Town of Cairo, Village of Castleton-on-the-Hudson, Village of Catskill, Town of Coeymans, Town of Cohoes, Town of Colonie, Columbia County, Dalton, Town of East Greenbush, City of Glens Falls, Village of Green Island, Village of Hudson Falls, City of Mechanicville, Village of Menands, City of Poughkeepsie, Village of Ravena, City of Rensselaer, City of Saratoga Springs, Town of Schodack, Village of Schuylerville, Village of Stillwater, City of Troy and Village of Waterford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 94 was closed west of Rt. 208 in Washingtonville due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Nor'easter traveled across Cape Cod into the Gulf of Maine on January 12th with portions of northern New York on the northwest outskirts of this storm. ||This storm had a long, dangerous and devastating wintry history from the southern Plains and Gulf coast states on January 9th to the southeast United States on January 10th, then coastal mid-Atlantic on January 11th until impacting the northeast late January 11th into January 12th.||Snow began across portions of northern New York around daybreak and continued nearly until midnight. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of snow fell across eastern Essex county, with some specific snowfall totals including; 8 inches in Ticonderoga and 7 inches in Moriah.

Weak low pressure tracked up the coast Tuesday and then just southeast of Long Island Tuesday night, bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the region. With cold air in place initially, light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening. Then as warmer air began to work into the region aloft late Tuesday night into early Wednesday morning, the precipitation changed to sleet and eventually freezing rain, before ending as a mix of freezing rain and rain Wednesday afternoon. Between 2 and 4 inches of snow and sleet fell across the region Tuesday night, with one quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulation on top of that. This made for dangerous road conditions for the Wednesday morning commute.

The combination of a strong upper level disturbance and a slow moving inverted surface trough extending back over the region from offshore low pressure brought an unusual snow event to the region on Friday, January seventh. Light snow developed across the region during the morning, with a narrow band of heavy snow developing  by early Friday afternoon. This band of heavy snow, with snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour, then slowly translated east across Southern Connecticut late Friday afternoon. Snow totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches across northeastern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley.

The combination of a strong upper level disturbance and a slow moving inverted surface trough extending back over the region from offshore low pressure brought an unusual snow event to the region on Friday, January seventh. Light snow developed across the region during the morning, with a narrow band of heavy snow developing  by early Friday afternoon. This band of heavy snow, with snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour, then slowly translated east across Southern Connecticut late Friday afternoon. Snow totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches across northeastern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley.

A period of moderate to heavy snow overspread the region Wednesday morning into Wednesday afternoon on January 26th in response to a subtropical moisture feed ahead of a Mid Atlantic low pressure system. This bout of snow produced 2 to 5 inches across the region. The snow tapered to a light wintry mix late Wednesday afternoon into early evening, before a second heavier round of precipitation with convection worked into the region Wednesday evening. This was in response to a strong upper level disturbance working towards the region and rapidly intensifying coastal low pressure southeast of the region throwing moisture back over the area. A very heavy snow band developed over the NYC Metro, Southern and Eastern Portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Northern and Western Long Island Wednesday evening, which gradually moved northeast late Wednesday night. This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour over a 4 to 6 hr period,  raising snow totals to 15-20 inches across much of the region. Lower snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches were experienced over Eastern Long Island due to sleet mixing in with the snow for a time Wednesday night.||Over 19 inches of snow accumulated in Central Park. Mayor Michael Bloomberg declared a weather emergency for New York City. Bloomberg announced the closure of all non-emergency city government offices and New York City Schools were closed. The storm broke New York���s record for January snowfall. A total of 36 inches had fallen in Central Park this month compared with 27.4 inches in 1925.||Snow accumulations caused massive transportation delays including over a thousand airline cancellations at all of the major New York airports. John F. Kennedy International airport was closed until 10:30 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. Amtrak suspended its passenger rail service from New York to Boston and cut service between New York and Albany.

Weak low pressure tracked up the coast Tuesday and then just southeast of Long Island Tuesday night, bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the region. With cold air in place initially, light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening. Then as warmer air began to work into the region aloft late Tuesday night into early Wednesday morning, the precipitation changed to sleet and eventually freezing rain, before ending as a mix of freezing rain and rain Wednesday afternoon. Between 2 and 4 inches of snow and sleet fell across the region Tuesday night, with one quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulation on top of that. This made for dangerous road conditions for the Wednesday morning commute.

A period of moderate to heavy snow overspread the region Wednesday morning into Wednesday afternoon on January 26th in response to a subtropical moisture feed ahead of a Mid Atlantic low pressure system. This bout of snow produced 2 to 5 inches across the region. The snow tapered to a light wintry mix late Wednesday afternoon into early evening, before a second heavier round of precipitation with convection worked into the region Wednesday evening. This was in response to a strong upper level disturbance working towards the region and rapidly intensifying coastal low pressure southeast of the region throwing moisture back over the area. A very heavy snow band developed over the NYC Metro, Southern and Eastern Portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Northern and Western Long Island Wednesday evening, which gradually moved northeast late Wednesday night. This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour over a 4 to 6 hr period,  raising snow totals to 15-20 inches across much of the region. Lower snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches were experienced over Eastern Long Island due to sleet mixing in with the snow for a time Wednesday night.||Over 19 inches of snow accumulated in Central Park. Mayor Michael Bloomberg declared a weather emergency for New York City. Bloomberg announced the closure of all non-emergency city government offices and New York City Schools were closed. The storm broke New York���s record for January snowfall. A total of 36 inches had fallen in Central Park this month compared with 27.4 inches in 1925.||Snow accumulations caused massive transportation delays including over a thousand airline cancellations at all of the major New York airports. John F. Kennedy International airport was closed until 10:30 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. Amtrak suspended its passenger rail service from New York to Boston and cut service between New York and Albany.

This lake effect storm evolved from a departing synoptic system which gave most of western and central New York several inches of snow overnight on the 20th-21st. A brisk westerly flow followed with bands of lake snow setting up during Friday afternoon (21st) and continuing into the evening before dissipating after midnight as a ridge moved in. Off Lake Ontario, the heaviest snows fell over Oswego County where the activity was focused.  Specific storm totals off Lake Ontario included: 17��� at Minetto; 12��� at Palermo and Fulton; 10��� at Scriba; and 8��� at Martville and Harrisville. Off Lake Erie storm totals included: 13��� at Colden; 11��� at Springville and 7��� at East Aurora and Forestville.

Warm air spread over northern New York on March 17 and 18. Runoff from snowmelt caused rivers to rise, and some of the remaining ice cover on northern rivers broke up and created ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Great Chazy River moved into Perry Mills...and water approached nearby roadways at 1830 EDT on the 18th. Water backed up behind the jam, flooding basements, surrounding homes, and flooding fields. At 1230 EDT on March 19 water levels behind the jam rose suddenly, forcing evacuations and flooding Creek Road. The jam moved out of the Perry Mills area at 2030 pm on March 19, but additional ice jam flooding developed downstream on the Great Chazy in Champlain Village. Water finally receded the morning of March 21 in Champlain Village.

Light to moderate rain associated with a warm front late on April 10 was preceded by several days of above freezing temperatures and melting snow. On April 11 a cluster of thunderstorms moved west to east across northern New York during the early morning hours. Flooding resulted from the combination of snowmelt and rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 190, The Military Turnpike, had water flowing over the road near Duley Road. Flooding was also reported on Bradley Pond Road in Ellenburg and Bowen Road in Saranac. In Black Brook, Bonnieview Road and Forest Dale roads were closed due to washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system approached from the Great Lakes region Friday night, January 7th, while a secondary area of low pressure formed along the mid Atlantic coast. These lows moved slowly eastward Friday night into Saturday, eventually consolidating into one low during the day Saturday, January 8th. Three to 15 inches of snow fell across east central New York.

A persistent westerly flow of cold air across Lake Ontario produced bands of heavy lake effect snow. Twelve-hour snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 10 inches across the northern and central portion of Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve-hour snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 10 inches across the northern and central portion of Oneida County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system sitting over eastern Canada brought persistent northwest flow to central New York. Cold air crossing Lake Ontario brought heavy lake effect snow to the snow-belt areas. Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 12 inches across Madison, Chenango and Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 12 inches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure built across the eastern Great Lakes region and brought bitter cold temperatures to the eastern Lake Ontario Region. Morning lows ranged from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees. Even though the winds were relatively light, wind chill temperatures reached minus 40 in some locations.

Bitterly cold air settled into the region as Canadian high pressure built in. Temperatures plummeted to 5 to 30 degrees below zero across east central New York. Brisk westerly winds diminished during the evening, becoming light and variable to calm after midnight. The winds resulted in wind chill readings of 10 to 40 degrees below zero.||Numerous schools were closed or had delayed starts across east central New York due to the extreme cold. ||Amtrak temporarily suspended service between Albany and New York City because the extreme cold caused some signals, switches and equipment to freeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a surface cold front moved through central New York and into the western Catskills during the morning hours on January 7th. Snow overspread the area during the early morning hours on Friday January 7th, and continued into Friday evening. The snow became heavy by afternoon over Sullivan County, as a secondary area of low pressure developed over southeast New York. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 8 inches across southern Sullivan County, with 7.4 inches at Kiamesha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulations across the southern part of the county ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Kiamesha measured 7.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Great Lakes with light snow spreading into central New York and northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon hours on Tuesday January 11th. Another low pressure system tracked northeast along the East Coast during the overnight hours on Wednesday January 12th. This deepening low pressure system produced steadier snow across Sullivan County Wednesday morning. Snowfall totals around 7 inches were recorded near Bloomingburg with 7.2 inches and Neversink with 6.5 inches by daybreak Wednesday. The remainder of the county recorded less than 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals around 7 inches were recorded near Bloomingburg with 7.2 inches and Neversink with 6.5 inches by daybreak Wednesday. The remainder of the county recorded less than 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent northwest flow accompanied by a trough of low pressure brought significant lake effect snow to upstate New York. Three hour snowfall amounts of nearly 7 inches were recorded in Cazenovia during this storm. Snowfall totals ranged from 7.7 inches in Clay in Onondaga County to 10.5 inches at Point Rock in northern Oneida County. Snowfall totals of 7 to 10 inches were common across northern Onondaga, northern Madison, and central and northern Oneida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the northern part of the county ranged from 5 to 11 inches. Point Rock measured 10.5 inches.

Snowfall totals of 7 to 10 inches were common across northern Onondaga, northern Madison, and central and northern Oneida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the northern part of the county ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent northwest flow accompanied by a trough of low pressure brought significant lake effect snow to upstate New York. Three hour snowfall amounts of nearly 7 inches were recorded in Cazenovia during this storm. Snowfall totals ranged from 7.7 inches in Clay in Onondaga County to 10.5 inches at Point Rock in northern Oneida County. Snowfall totals of 7 to 10 inches were common across northern Onondaga, northern Madison, and central and northern Oneida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the northern part of the county ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Canadian Maritimes provided persistent northwesterly flow to Upstate New York during the period. Cold air flowing across Lake Ontario resulted in lake effect snow across the snow-belts of Oneida county. Lake effect snowfall totals of 7 inches fell north of the New York Thruway corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches fell north of the New York Thruway corridor. Durhamville measured 10.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of moderate to heavy snow overspread the region Wednesday morning into Wednesday afternoon on January 26th in response to a subtropical moisture feed ahead of a Mid Atlantic low pressure system. This bout of snow produced 2 to 5 inches across the region. The snow tapered to a light wintry mix late Wednesday afternoon into early evening, before a second heavier round of precipitation with convection worked into the region Wednesday evening. This was in response to a strong upper level disturbance working towards the region and rapidly intensifying coastal low pressure southeast of the region throwing moisture back over the area. A very heavy snow band developed over the NYC Metro, Southern and Eastern Portions of the Lower Hudson Valley and Northern and Western Long Island Wednesday evening, which gradually moved northeast late Wednesday night. This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour over a 4 to 6 hr period,  raising snow totals to 15-20 inches across much of the region. Lower snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches were experienced over Eastern Long Island due to sleet mixing in with the snow for a time Wednesday night.||Over 19 inches of snow accumulated in Central Park. Mayor Michael Bloomberg declared a weather emergency for New York City. Bloomberg announced the closure of all non-emergency city government offices and New York City Schools were closed. The storm broke New York���s record for January snowfall. A total of 36 inches had fallen in Central Park this month compared with 27.4 inches in 1925.||Snow accumulations caused massive transportation delays including over a thousand airline cancellations at all of the major New York airports. John F. Kennedy International airport was closed until 10:30 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. Amtrak suspended its passenger rail service from New York to Boston and cut service between New York and Albany.

As the upper level disturbance worked over the region late Tuesday night a semi-stationary north to south band of very heavy snow developed just east of the Hudson River into the New York City metropolitan area, gradually pivoting east Wednesday morning through early Wednesday afternoon. Light to moderate rain associated with a warm front late on April 10 was preceded by several days of above freezing temperatures and melting snow. On April 11 a cluster of thunderstorms moved west to east across northern New York during the early morning hours. Flooding resulted from the combination of snowmelt and rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive runoff from rain and snowmelt flooded 2 to 3 homes near Hatch Brook and on Studley Hill Road.

Weak low pressure tracked up the coast Tuesday and then just southeast of Long Island Tuesday night, bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain to the region. With cold air in place initially, light snow overspread the region Tuesday evening. Then as warmer air began to work into the region aloft late Tuesday night into early Wednesday morning, the precipitation changed to sleet and eventually freezing rain, before ending as a mix of freezing rain and rain Wednesday afternoon. Between 2 and 4 inches of snow and sleet fell across the region Tuesday night, with one quarter to one half of an inch of ice accumulation on top of that. This made for dangerous road conditions for the Wednesday morning commute.

An arctic cold front moved across the region early in the morning Saturday, February 19th. Lake enhanced snow showers occurred in the Mohawk Valley in the wake of the boundary as the cold air moved over the relatively warmer waters of Lake Erie. These snow showers in combination with very strong and gusty west to northwest resulted in white out conditions at times and the closure of the New York State Thruway in both directions between Exit 28 (Fultonville-Fonda) to Exit 31 (Utica).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New York State Thruway Authority closed Interstate 90 in both directions from Exit 28 (Fultonville-Fonda) to Exit 31 (Utica) due to blowing and drifting of snow. In addition, Route 170 and Route 170A north of the Village of Little Falls was closed due to white out conditions.

This band was responsible for snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour over a 4 to 6 hr period,  raising snow totals to 15-20 inches across much of the region. Lower snowfall amounts of 8-12 inches were experienced over Eastern Long Island due to sleet mixing in with the snow for a time Wednesday night.||Over 19 inches of snow accumulated in Central Park. Mayor Michael Bloomberg declared a weather emergency for New York City. Bloomberg announced the closure of all non-emergency city government offices and New York City Schools were closed. The storm broke New York���s record for January snowfall. A total of 36 inches had fallen in Central Park this month compared with 27.4 inches in 1925.||Snow accumulations caused massive transportation delays including over a thousand airline cancellations at all of the major New York airports. John F. Kennedy International airport was closed until 10:30 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. Amtrak suspended its passenger rail service from New York to Boston and cut service between New York and Albany.

A weak surface low across the Great Lakes moved across New York and New England on the night of February 7th and morning of February 8th. At the same time, a coastal low that originated in the lower Mississippi, moved well southeast of Cape Cod. ||Snow overspread the region during the late evening of February 7th and tapered off during the midday hours of February 8th. Snowfall amounts across northern New York were generally 3 to 6 inches.

On February 9th, a weak surface trough along with a broad west-southwest, cold cyclonic lower atmospheric flow across Lake Ontario accounted for a single lake effect snow band from Lake Ontario into portions of extreme southern St. Lawrence county.||Snowfall totals were 2 to 6 inches in southern St. Lawrence county with localized reports of a foot at Gouverneur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In April, the combination of several rounds of heavy rainfall from convection, rapid snowmelt due to the warm temperatures and above normal soil moisture led to increased runoff and rapid river rises. Starting on April 28th widespread flooding of rivers, streams and creeks occurred resulting in road closures and flooding of properties. The flooding continued into the first part of May along the Hudson River, Schroon River and along the shores of Great Sacandaga Lake. ||Along the Hudson River, the river gauge at Fort Edward finally fell below flood stage on early in the morning May 6th, Schuylerville late in the evening on May 9th, Mechanicville late in the evening on May 2nd, and Troy early in the morning on May 1st. The river gauge at Riverbank on Schroon River fell below flood stage early in the morning on May 7th. Along the Sacandaga River, the river gauge at Stewarts Bridge in Hadley fell below flood stage late in the morning on May 2nd and the Sacandaga Reservior late in the evening on May 6th.||The New York State Canal System remained closed until late May due to high water levels, excessive flow and floating debris. On May 25th, the Erie Canal from Lock E-2 (Waterford) to Lock E-23 (Brewerton) and the Champlain Canal from C-1 (Waterford) to Lock C-12 (Whitehall) finally opened.||Refer to April 2011 Storm Events Database for more details on the extent of the flooding and damage estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding which began on April 28th continued along the Schroon River in Warren County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in warm, humid air as a cold front approached the region during the afternoon hours.  The thunderstorms produced wind gusts measured to 52 knots. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal to record precipitation of 5 to 9 inches occurred in April across a large area of New York State. There was above normal snowpack across the higher elevations of the Adirondacks with snow depths at least a foot above normal. A warm front lifted northward across the area on Tuesday, April 26th, ushering in a warm and humid airmass. Temperatures rose into the 70s and 80s across the area on Tuesday and again on Wednesday, April 27th (15 to 25 degrees above normal). In addition, overnight lows were mild. ||An area of heavy showers and embedded thunderstorms accompanied the warm front as it moved through on Tuesday, April 26th, impacting areas mainly north and west of the Capital District. The boundary became stationary near the Saint Lawrence River Valley and northern New England. A disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere and an area of low pressure moving towards the Great Lakes Region focused another round of showers and thunderstorms that impacted the western and central Mohawk Valley, Adirondack region, and the Upper Hudson River Valley including the Lake George Region late Wednesday afternoon into the early evening, April 27th. Yet another wave of low pressure moved into the eastern Great Lakes Region Thursday morning, April 28th. This low intensified as it approached and had strong upper level support favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms producing severe weather and very heavy rainfall moved over already saturated ground to the north and west of the Capital District during the morning hours. ||The combination of several rounds of heavy rainfall from convection, rapid snowmelt due to the warm temperatures and above normal soil moisture led to increased runoff and rapid river rises. Widespread flooding of rivers, streams and creeks occurred resulting in road closures and flooding of properties. On Thursday, April 28th, an earthen dam failed sending water, dirt and debris from Lewiston Pond on Route 9 at Brower Road into the Longberry Lake which is Saratoga Springs' drinking water supply (no health threat report). Many record crests occurred on rivers, creeks and streams throughout the upper Hudson and upper Mohawk basins. ||The following river points reached record levels:|Hudson River at North Creek in Warren County, Fort Edwards in Washington/Saratoga counties and Schuylerville in Saratoga/Washington counties.|Stillwater Reservior at Beaver River in Herkmier County.|Great Sacandaga River at Conklingville and Stewarts Bridge near Hadley in Saratoga County.|West Canada Creek near Wilmurt and Kast Bridge in Herkimer County.|Indian River near Indian Lake in Hamilton County.|Hinkley Reservior at Hinkley in Herkimer County. ||As a result of the flooding, states of emergency were declared in Indian Lake and Long Lake Hamilton County, Town of Johnsburg Warren/Hamilton Counties, Town of Chester Warren County, and in the Village of Middleville Herkimer County. ||The following school districts were closed on April 29th due to the flooding: Indian Lake and Long Lake in Hamilton County and Johnsburg in Warren County.||The New York State Canal Corporation delayed the opening of the New York State Canal System due to high water and excessive flows.||Disaster damage estimates from the flooding and some severe weather in April 2011 total $2,622,025 for the following counties in east central New York.|Hamilton:	 $481,000|Herkimer: $571,800|Saratoga: unknown|Warren:   $676,225|Washington: $53,000||On June 10th, The U.S. Department of Homeland Security's Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) announced that federal disaster assistance had been made available to the state of New York to supplement state and local recovery efforts in the area struck by storms, flooding, tornadoes, and straight-line winds during the period of April 26 to May 8, 2011. This includes the counties of Hamilton, Herkimer, and Warren in east central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Hudson River in Warren County from North River southward to the Saratoga County line. Numerous towns reported flooding of roadways, houses and riverside camps. Some properties had several feet of water in them. The following major thoroughfares were closed due to flooding Thursday, April 28th, well into Friday, April 29th: East River Drive in Lake Luzerne, Route 28 and 28N in the Town of Johnsburg and Route 418 in Thurman. The river gauge at North Creek on the Hudson River reached a record crest of 13.65 feet early Friday morning (flood stage is 10 feet). ||The North Creek Trailer Park on Route 28 in the Town of Johnsburg was evacuated Thursday afternoon, April 28th, because water from the Hudson River had entered the park. A mudslide in excess of 200 feet occurred on 13th Lake Road in North River/North Creek. In North Creek, a couple of hundred feet of railway tracks were reported under  2 to 5 feet of water on Thursday, with several buildings in the train station also flooded. In Stony Creek, the 1,000 Acres golf course flooded with the 9th green under 8 feet of water Thursday morning, April 28th. Flood waters receded through Saturday, April 30th.

Broad southwest flow ahead of the system allowed temperatures to warm into the lower 80s, and with moderate instability, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed. Many reports of large hail and damaging winds occurred in central New York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in warm, humid air as a cold front approached the region during the afternoon hours.  The thunderstorms produced wind gusts measured to 52 knots. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in Findley Lake, Clymer, Panama and Busti. Training thunderstorms brought heavy rains with radar estimated rainfall rates of two inches per hour. The Village of Lakewood suffered flash flooding. Numerous streets were closed by high water and basements were flooded.

In April, the combination of several rounds of heavy rainfall from convection, rapid snowmelt due to the warm temperatures and above normal soil moisture led to increased runoff and rapid river rises. Starting on April 28th widespread flooding of rivers, streams and creeks occurred resulting in road closures and flooding of properties. The flooding continued into the first part of May along the Hudson River, Schroon River and along the shores of Great Sacandaga Lake. ||Along the Hudson River, the river gauge at Fort Edward finally fell below flood stage on early in the morning May 6th, Schuylerville late in the evening on May 9th, Mechanicville late in the evening on May 2nd, and Troy early in the morning on May 1st. The river gauge at Riverbank on Schroon River fell below flood stage early in the morning on May 7th. Along the Sacandaga River, the river gauge at Stewarts Bridge in Hadley fell below flood stage late in the morning on May 2nd and the Sacandaga Reservior late in the evening on May 6th.||The New York State Canal System remained closed until late May due to high water levels, excessive flow and floating debris. On May 25th, the Erie Canal from Lock E-2 (Waterford) to Lock E-23 (Brewerton) and the Champlain Canal from C-1 (Waterford) to Lock C-12 (Whitehall) finally opened.||Refer to April 2011 Storm Events Database for more details on the extent of the flooding and damage estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding which began on April 28th continued along the Hudson River in Washington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal to record precipitation of 5 to 9 inches occurred in April across a large area of New York State. There was above normal snowpack across the higher elevations of the Adirondacks with snow depths at least a foot above normal. A warm front lifted northward across the area on Tuesday, April 26th, ushering in a warm and humid airmass. Temperatures rose into the 70s and 80s across the area on Tuesday and again on Wednesday, April 27th (15 to 25 degrees above normal). In addition, overnight lows were mild. ||An area of heavy showers and embedded thunderstorms accompanied the warm front as it moved through on Tuesday, April 26th, impacting areas mainly north and west of the Capital District. The boundary became stationary near the Saint Lawrence River Valley and northern New England. A disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere and an area of low pressure moving towards the Great Lakes Region focused another round of showers and thunderstorms that impacted the western and central Mohawk Valley, Adirondack region, and the Upper Hudson River Valley including the Lake George Region late Wednesday afternoon into the early evening, April 27th. Yet another wave of low pressure moved into the eastern Great Lakes Region Thursday morning, April 28th. This low intensified as it approached and had strong upper level support favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms producing severe weather and very heavy rainfall moved over already saturated ground to the north and west of the Capital District during the morning hours. ||The combination of several rounds of heavy rainfall from convection, rapid snowmelt due to the warm temperatures and above normal soil moisture led to increased runoff and rapid river rises. Widespread flooding of rivers, streams and creeks occurred resulting in road closures and flooding of properties. On Thursday, April 28th, an earthen dam failed sending water, dirt and debris from Lewiston Pond on Route 9 at Brower Road into the Longberry Lake which is Saratoga Springs' drinking water supply (no health threat report). This includes the counties of Hamilton, Herkimer, and Warren in east central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Sacandaga River and Hudson River in Saratoga County. Numerous roadways, houses and riverside camps were flooded. Some properties had several feet of water in them. Part of a house in Hadley was washed away Thursday evening, April 28th. In Moreau, residents were cut off from their houses Friday morning when water from the Hudson River quickly covered Old Bend Road. National Grid shut off power to the neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across the Great Lakes bringing moist, unstable air to the region. Showers and thunderstorms developed along lake breeze boundaries and persisted through the late afternoon and evening hours across the western southern tier and western Finger Lakes Region.  Localized rainfall amounts of up to three inches fell in just a few hours resulting in flash flooding. Numerous roads were closed and/or damaged by the flood waters. Basements were flooded. In some locations, for example Wellsville, States of Emergency were declared in response to the flooding. In Scio, one woman suffered minor injuries as a section of Iron Road gave way as she was driving on it. About a dozen homes were evacuated in Wellsville. Several golf courses sustained damage from the flood waters including Attica, Bethany Hills and Byrncliff. Specific roads closed included:  Gerry-Levant, Mee and Stone Roads in Falconer, County Road 48 in Belmont, Routes 9 and 31 in Scio, Chaddock Road in Attica, Perry Road in Sheldon, Route 15 in Geneseo, and Route 16 between Yorkshire and Delevan.  Several farms suffered crop damage as recently planted seed was washed away by the flood waters.

Heavy rain and snowmelt combined to produce significant flooding in the northern Adirondacks. Rivers were running high due to melt from an above normal snowpack, then two to three inches of rain fell across the area on April 26 and 27. An additional one to two inches of rain fell on May 3 and 4, prolonging the flood event. Water flowing out of the lakes regions of the Adirondacks were slow to rise and slow to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain and snowmelt caused flooding in Saranac Lake Village. Flooding was compounded by upstream releases from locks on Oseetah Lake, which flooded downstream Lake Flower at Saranac Lake Village. Businesses and homes were evacuated, and bridges and the village wastewater treatment plant were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain and snowmelt caused flooding on the Raquette River. The river gage on the Raquette at South Colton New York exceeded its flood stage of 10.0 feet at 16:02 EST on April 28, and crested at 11.27 feet at 04:45 EST on April 29. The gage on the Raquette at Piercefield exceeded its flood stage of 12.5 feet at 00:07 April 29, and crested on May 1 at 09:15 EST. There was widespread flooding of low lying property, homes and businesses along the Raquette through Piercefield, South Colton, Colton, Potsdam, and Norwood. Flooding continued into May.
